rationale: Integration.GetMovementUpdates filters Warehouse.StockItemTransactions on a SARGable date-range predicate (LastEditedWhen > @LastCutoff AND <= @NewCutoff) but the table has NO index on LastEditedWhen (only the clustered PK on StockItemTransactionID + 6 single-column FK indexes), so every call does a full clustered-index scan of ~236,667 rows regardless of how narrow the cutoff window is. Per the SQL Server index design guide, this is the canonical case for a COVERING index: 'Consider covering indexes when retrieving a small portion of a large table, and where that small portion is defined by a fixed predicate.' Column order follows the guide: the filter column (LastEditedWhen) is the key; the columns the SELECT projects are INCLUDE (non-key) columns so the query is fully covered with no key lookups; StockItemTransactionID is the clustered key and is auto-included in every nonclustered index. This converts the frequent narrow-window incremental-ETL calls from a 236k-row scan into a tight range seek. The predicate is already SARGable (column alone on one side), so no rewrite is needed — the change is expressible purely as an index. Citations: https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/relational-databases/sql-server-index-design-guide?view=sql-server-ver17#nonclustered-index-design-guidelines and https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/relational-databases/indexes/create-indexes-with-included-columns?view=sql-server-ver17 ; SARGability confirmed at https://learn.microsoft.com/troubleshoot/sql/database-engine/performance/troubleshoot-high-cpu-usage-issues#step-6-investigate-and-resolve-sargability-issues
apply_sql:
CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_StockItemTransactions_LastEditedWhen_Covering
ON Warehouse.StockItemTransactions (LastEditedWhen)
INCLUDE (TransactionOccurredWhen, InvoiceID, PurchaseOrderID, Quantity,
         StockItemID, CustomerID, SupplierID, TransactionTypeID);
rollback_sql: DROP INDEX IF EXISTS IX_StockItemTransactions_LastEditedWhen_Covering ON Warehouse.StockItemTransactions;

rationale: After the covering index, the actual plans still show a parameter-sniffing tail: the cached seek+sort plan is compiled (sniffed) on the first/narrow parameter set (~293 rows), so its memory grant is sized for that, and reusing it for the wide full-reload windows makes the ORDER BY sort spill to tempdb (Sort Warning) with >10x estimate skew. Per the Query Processing Architecture Guide, 'OPTION (RECOMPILE) ... allows the optimizer to generate a more optimal query plan tailored to the specific values ... improve performance for queries with highly variable or skewed input data.' An Integration.Get*Updates ETL proc runs periodically (not in rapid succession) over highly variable date windows, so a per-call compile is cheap relative to the gain and right-sizes both the plan and the memory grant per call -- removing the spill and skew (verified: narrow combos seek, full reloads use an already-ordered clustered scan with no sort spill). Citations: https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/relational-databases/query-processing-architecture-guide?view=sql-server-ver17#execution-plan-caching-and-reuse ; https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/relational-databases/post-migration-validation-and-optimization-guide?view=sql-server-ver17#sensitivity-to-parameter-sniffing ; sort-spill mechanics: https://learn.microsoft.com/sql/relational-databases/memory-management-architecture-guide?view=sql-server-ver17#effects-of-min-memory-per-query
apply_sql:
ALTER PROCEDURE Integration.GetMovementUpdates_opt_v2
@LastCutoff datetime2(7),
@NewCutoff datetime2(7)
WITH EXECUTE AS OWNER
AS
BEGIN
    SET NOCOUNT ON;
    SET XACT_ABORT ON;

    SELECT CAST(sit.TransactionOccurredWhen AS date) AS [Date Key],
           sit.StockItemTransactionID AS [WWI Stock Item Transaction ID],
           sit.InvoiceID AS [WWI Invoice ID],
           sit.PurchaseOrderID AS [WWI Purchase Order ID],
           CAST(sit.Quantity AS int) AS Quantity,
           sit.StockItemID AS [WWI Stock Item ID],
           sit.CustomerID AS [WWI Customer ID],
           sit.SupplierID AS [WWI Supplier ID],
           sit.TransactionTypeID AS [WWI Transaction Type ID],
           sit.TransactionOccurredWhen AS [Transaction Occurred When]
    FROM Warehouse.StockItemTransactions AS sit
    WHERE sit.LastEditedWhen > @LastCutoff
    AND sit.LastEditedWhen <= @NewCutoff
    ORDER BY sit.StockItemTransactionID
    OPTION (RECOMPILE);

    RETURN 0;
END;
